wallet_rationale: One-time supplemental appropriation for FY2027; provides additional funding beyond the regular budget that may cover state employee pay adjustments, benefit costs, or agency operational needs

synopsis: This Act requires long-term care facilities to have insurance policies that provide a minimum of $1 million per claim/$3 million aggregate coverage each for general liability and professional liability. This Act takes effect 180 days after enactment.
plain_english: Nursing homes and long-term care facilities in Delaware must now carry at least $1 million in liability insurance per claim — meaning if something goes wrong during your care or a family member's care, there's a larger pool of money available to cover damages and legal claims.
wallet_rationale: Amends long-term care rules under Title 16; changes to facility requirements, eligibility standards, or cost structures directly affect the financial exposure of seniors relying on nursing home or assisted living services

synopsis: This Act expands the scope of prohibited extraordinary collection actions to include a large health-care facility levying on tangible personal property of a patient. It also clarifies that any party taking action to collect medical debt must disclose in court pleadings whether the party is a large health-care facility, and makes technical corrections.
plain_english: Large hospitals can no longer seize your personal property — your car, furniture, or other belongings — to collect an unpaid medical bill, and any collector trying to sue you over medical debt must now disclose whether a hospital is behind the claim.

veteran.rationale: Prohibits hospitals from placing liens on primary residences or garnishing income for medical debt; veterans with gaps in VA coverage who receive civilian care face the highest risk of unpaid medical bills that this law directly shields against.

wallet_rationale: Medical Debt Protection Act restricts the collection and credit reporting of medical debt, directly shielding individuals from debt-driven financial ruin; seniors — who face higher medical costs — and low-wage workers with high out-of-pocket expenses benefit most

synopsis: Chapter 35 of Title 19 prohibits an employer from improperly classifying an individual who is an employee as an independent contractor. Not only is this improper classification unfair to employees because it violates state and federal laws related to income tax withholding, unemployment insurance, wage laws, and workers' compensation, it is also unfair to contractors who comply with Chapter 35 and these other employment laws. When employers who violate Chapter 35 are subcontractors who have not registered as contractors as required under Chapter 36 of Title 19, the Department of Labor (Department) has no recourse for enforcing compliance with Chapter 35. This Act makes a general contractor responsible for a subcontractor's compliance with Chapter 35 by making the general contractor jointly and severally liable for restitution and penalties assessed against the subcontractor. This Act also allows the Department to deny, suspend, or revoke the certificate of registration of a contractor who contracts with a subcontractor who has not registered on any project. In addition, Section 3 of this Act makes technical corrections to conform existing law to the standards of the Delaware Legislative Drafting Manual.
plain_english: If you've been misclassified as an independent contractor when you're really an employee, this law (passed over the governor's veto) makes the general contractor on a project jointly responsible for a subcontractor's wage and classification violations — so you have a better shot at recovering back wages, tax withholding, and workers' comp you were owed.
wallet_rationale: Labor law amendment (veto overridden, signaling significant employer opposition) to Title 19; veto-override status indicates a substantive worker protection — likely expanding paid leave, wage theft remedies, or minimum wage indexing — that imposes new financial obligations on employers

synopsis: This Act limits the restrictions a county may impose on the operation of marijuana establishments, including: requiring that a medical marijuana compassion center granted a conversion license for a retail marijuana store be allowed to operate as a nonconforming use; prohibiting denial of a building permit if improvements comply with physical zoning requirements; requiring counties to allow minimum operating hours of 9 a.m.-9 p.m. Monday-Saturday and noon-8 p.m. Sunday; permitting indoor, fully-enclosed cultivation facilities in agricultural/industrial zones; and allowing retail marijuana stores in commercial/industrial zones to be restricted only within ½ mile of another store or within 500 feet of a place of worship, school, licensed child care, residential treatment facility, park, or library. These limits preempt and supersede existing and future county ordinances regarding marijuana establishments. This Act also makes corresponding and technical changes to align language regarding municipalities.
plain_english: Counties in Delaware can no longer block or over-restrict licensed marijuana stores and cultivation facilities through local ordinances — if your cannabis business meets state licensing and zoning rules, counties must allow you to operate with standard hours and cannot deny your building permits.
wallet_rationale: Limits county authority to restrict marijuana retail operations, including overriding building permit denials for licensed cannabis retailers; creates regulatory uncertainty and compliance complexity for cannabis business operators
